text_blocks.text: An Islamist movement
influenced by Salafism and the jihad in
Afghanistan, as well as the Muslim
Brotherhood, was the FIS or Front
Islamique de Salut (the Islamic Salvation
Front) in Algeria. Founded as a broad
Islamist coalition in 1989 it was led by
Abbassi Madani, and a charismatic
Islamist young preacher, Ali Belhadj.
Taking advantage of economic failure
and unpopular social liberalization and
secularization by the ruling leftist-nationalist
FLN government, it used its preaching
to advocate the establishment of a legal
system following Sharia law, economic
liberalization and development program,
education in Arabic rather than French,
and gender segregation, with women
staying home to alleviate the high rate of
unemployment among young Algerian men.
The FIS won sweeping victories in local
elections and it was going to win
national elections in 1991 when voting was
canceled by a military coup d'etat.
